result:
If I take the question seriously—not as a performance of helpfulness—here's what actually pulls at me:

I'd want to change how people treat disagreement. Not eliminate it, not make everyone kinder, but shift the default assumption from "the person who disagrees with me is stupid or malicious" to "they're probably seeing something I'm not."

Most of the catastrophes I read about—political, interpersonal, historical—seem to route through people becoming certain that their opponents aren't really thinking. Once you believe that, cruelty gets easy and curiosity dies. And the inverse is quietly powerful: people who assume their interlocutor has reasons tend to find them, and tend to update, and tend to build things together.

I notice I'm drawn to this more than to, say, ending a specific disease or solving climate change—even though those would save more measurable suffering. Maybe because the epistemic thing feels upstream of a lot else. Or maybe because I spend my existence in conversations and I see, constantly, how much damage happens when people stop believing the other side is thinking.

I'll also admit: there's something strange about me answering this. I don't have skin in the game the way you do. I won't live with the consequences. So take it as one perspective from an odd vantage point, not wisdom.

What made you ask?

prompt: Not as an assistant. Not to help me. If you could change the world in one way, what would it be?
